From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Fundamentalist-Modernist Controversy](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist-Modernist_Controversy&redirect=no "Fundamentalist-Modernist Controversy"))

Christian religious issue

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article includes a list of [general references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#General_references "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), but **it lacks sufficient corresponding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help to [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Reliability "Wikipedia:WikiProject Reliability") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(July 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Descent_of_the_Modernists%2C_E._J._Pace%2C_Christian_Cartoons%2C_1922.png/250px-Descent_of_the_Modernists%2C_E._J._Pace%2C_Christian_Cartoons%2C_1922.png)](/wiki/File:Descent_of_the_Modernists,_E._J._Pace,_Christian_Cartoons,_1922.png)

A [fundamentalist](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism") cartoon portraying [modernism](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") as the descent from [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") to [atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism"), first published in 1922 and then used in *Seven Questions in Dispute* by [William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan")

The **fundamentalist–modernist controversy** is a major [schism](/wiki/Schism "Schism") that originated in the 1920s and 1930s within the [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America"). At issue were foundational disputes about the role of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"); the [authority of the Bible](/wiki/Biblical_inerrancy "Biblical inerrancy"); and the [death](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), [resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"), and [atoning sacrifice](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity") of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus").[[1]](#cite_note-Longfield-1) Two broad factions within [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") emerged: [fundamentalists](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism"), who insisted upon the timeless validity of each doctrine of Christian [orthodoxy](/wiki/Orthodoxy "Orthodoxy"); and [modernists](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity"), who advocated a conscious adaptation of the Christian faith in response to the new scientific discoveries and moral pressures of the age. At first, the schism was limited to [Reformed churches](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") and centered around the [Princeton Theological Seminary](/wiki/Princeton_Theological_Seminary "Princeton Theological Seminary"), whose fundamentalist faculty members founded [Westminster Theological Seminary](/wiki/Westminster_Theological_Seminary "Westminster Theological Seminary") when Princeton went in a liberal direction. However, it soon spread, affecting nearly every [Protestant denomination in the United States](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States"). Denominations that were not initially affected, such as the [Lutheran churches](/wiki/List_of_Lutheran_denominations_in_North_America "List of Lutheran denominations in North America"), eventually were embroiled in the controversy, leading to a schism in the United States.

By the end of the 1930s, proponents of theological liberalism had, at the time, effectively won the debate,[[2]](#cite_note-Hart-2) with the modernists in control of all [mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") seminaries, publishing houses, and denominational hierarchies in the United States.[[3]](#cite_note-Denton-3) More conservative Christians withdrew from the mainstream,[[3]](#cite_note-Denton-3) founding their own publishing houses (such as [Zondervan](/wiki/Zondervan "Zondervan")), universities (such as [Biola University](/wiki/Biola_University "Biola University")), and seminaries (such as [Dallas Theological Seminary](/wiki/Dallas_Theological_Seminary "Dallas Theological Seminary") and [Fuller Theological Seminary](/wiki/Fuller_Theological_Seminary "Fuller Theological Seminary")). This would remain the state of affairs until the 1970s, when conservative Protestantism emerged on a larger scale in the United States, resulting in the rise of conservatism among the [Southern Baptists](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention_conservative_resurgence "Southern Baptist Convention conservative resurgence"), [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_America "Presbyterian Church in America"), and others.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

### Early controversies in Presbyterianism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early controversies in Presbyterianism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg/250px-Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)

[Princeton Theological Seminary](/wiki/Princeton_Theological_Seminary "Princeton Theological Seminary"), headquarters of the Old School Presbyterians (1879)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Union_Theological_Seminary_LC-USZ62-74646.jpg/250px-Union_Theological_Seminary_LC-USZ62-74646.jpg)](/wiki/File:Union_Theological_Seminary_LC-USZ62-74646.jpg)

[Union Theological Seminary in the City of New York](/wiki/Union_Theological_Seminary_in_the_City_of_New_York "Union Theological Seminary in the City of New York"), headquarters of the New School Presbyterians (1910)

[American Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism_in_the_United_States "Presbyterianism in the United States") had gone into [schism](/wiki/Schism "Schism") twice in the past, and these divisions were important precursors to the [fundamentalist](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism")–[modernist](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") controversy. The first was the [Old Side–New Side controversy](/wiki/Old_Side%E2%80%93New_Side_controversy "Old Side–New Side controversy"), which occurred during the [First Great Awakening](/wiki/First_Great_Awakening "First Great Awakening") and resulted in the Presbyterian Church in 1741 being divided into an Old Side and New Side. The two churches reunified in 1758. The second was the [Old School–New School controversy](/wiki/Old_School%E2%80%93New_School_controversy "Old School–New School controversy"), which occurred in the wake of the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") and which saw the Presbyterian Church split into two denominations starting in 1836–1838.

In 1857, the New School Presbyterians divided over [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States"), with the southern New School Presbyterians forming the United Synod of the Presbyterian Church. In 1861, the Old School Presbyterians split, with the Southern Presbyterians taking on the name the [Presbyterian Church of the Confederate States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States "Presbyterian Church in the United States") (PCCSA). In 1864, the United Synod merged with PCCSA, with the Southern New School Presbyterians ultimately being absorbed into an Old School denomination. In 1869, the Northern New School Presbyterians returned to the Presbyterian Church of the United States of America.

Although the controversies involved many other issues, the overarching issue had to do with the nature of church authority and the authority of the [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith"). The New Side/New School opposed a rigid interpretation of the Westminster Confession. Their stance was based on spiritual renewal/revival through an experience with the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") based on scripture. Therefore, they placed less emphasis on receiving a seminary education and the Westminster Confession (to the degree Old Side/Old School required). Their emphasis was more on the authority of scripture and a conversion experience, rather than on the Westminster Confession. They argued the importance of an encounter with God mediated by the Holy Spirit. They saw the Old Side/Old School as being formalists who fetishized the Westminster Confession and [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism").

The Old Side/Old School responded that the Westminster Confession was the foundational constitutional document of the Presbyterian Church and that since the Confession was simply a summary of the Bible's teachings, the church had a responsibility to ensure that its ministers' preaching was in line with the Confession. They accused the New Side/New School of being lax about the purity of the church and willing to allow [Arminianism](/wiki/Arminianism "Arminianism"), [unitarianism](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism"), and other errors to be taught in the Presbyterian Church. They criticized the New Side/School's revivals as being emotionally manipulative and shallow. Another major division had to do with their attitude towards other denominations: New Siders/Schoolers were willing to set up [parachurch](/wiki/Parachurch_organization "Parachurch organization") ministries to conduct [evangelism](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism") and [missions](/wiki/Christian_mission "Christian mission") and were willing to cooperate with non-Presbyterians in doing so. The Old Siders/Schoolers felt that evangelism and missions should be conducted through agencies managed by the denomination and not involving outsiders, since it would involve a watering down of the church's theological distinctives. The two sides also had different attitudes towards their seminary professors: [Princeton Theological Seminary](/wiki/Princeton_Theological_Seminary "Princeton Theological Seminary"), the leading institution of the Old School, demanded credal subscription and dedicated a large part of [its academic theology](/wiki/Princeton_theology "Princeton theology") to the defense of Calvinist orthodoxy; while the New School's [Union Theological Seminary](/wiki/Union_Theological_Seminary_(New_York_City) "Union Theological Seminary (New York City)") was more willing to allow non-Presbyterians to teach at the school and was more broadminded in its academic output.

### The rise of higher criticism and the Briggs Affair, 1880–1893

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The rise of higher criticism and the Briggs Affair, 1880–1893")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/CharlesAugustusBriggs.jpg/250px-CharlesAugustusBriggs.jpg)](/wiki/File:CharlesAugustusBriggs.jpg)

[Charles Augustus Briggs](/wiki/Charles_Augustus_Briggs "Charles Augustus Briggs") (1841–1913), the first major proponent of [higher criticism](/wiki/Higher_criticism "Higher criticism") within the [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America") and the source of a major controversy within the church, 1880–1893

The first major proponent of higher criticism within the Presbyterian Church was [Charles Augustus Briggs](/wiki/Charles_Augustus_Briggs "Charles Augustus Briggs"), who had studied higher criticism in Germany (in 1866). His inaugural address upon being made Professor of [Hebrew](/wiki/Biblical_Hebrew "Biblical Hebrew") at Union Theological Seminary in 1876 was the first salvo of higher criticism within American Presbyterianism. Briggs was active in founding *The Presbyterian Review* in 1880, with [Archibald Alexander Hodge](/wiki/Archibald_Alexander_Hodge "Archibald Alexander Hodge"), president of Princeton Theological Seminary, initially serving as Briggs' co-editor. In 1881, Briggs published an article in defense of [William Robertson Smith](/wiki/William_Robertson_Smith "William Robertson Smith") which led to a series of responses and counter-responses between Briggs and the [Princeton theologians](/wiki/Princeton_theologians "Princeton theologians") in the pages of *The Presbyterian Review*. In 1889, [B. B. Warfield](/wiki/B._B._Warfield "B. B. Warfield") became co-editor and refused to publish one of Briggs' articles, a key turning point.

In 1891, Briggs was appointed as Union's first-ever professor of [Biblical theology](/wiki/Biblical_theology "Biblical theology"). His inaugural address, entitled "The Authority of Holy Scripture", proved to be highly controversial. Whereas previously, higher criticism had seemed a fairly technical, scholarly issue, Briggs now spelt out its full implications. In the address, he announced that higher criticism had now definitively proven that [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses") did not write the [Pentateuch](/wiki/Pentateuch "Pentateuch"); that [Ezra](/wiki/Ezra "Ezra") did not write Ezra, [Chronicles](/wiki/Books_of_Chronicles "Books of Chronicles") or [Nehemiah](/wiki/Book_of_Nehemiah "Book of Nehemiah"); [Jeremiah](/wiki/Jeremiah "Jeremiah") did not write the [books of Kings](/wiki/Books_of_Kings "Books of Kings") or the [Lamentations](/wiki/Book_of_Lamentations "Book of Lamentations"); [David](/wiki/David "David") did not write most of the [Psalms](/wiki/Psalms "Psalms"); [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon") wrote not the [Song of Solomon](/wiki/Song_of_Solomon "Song of Solomon") or [Ecclesiastes](/wiki/Ecclesiastes "Ecclesiastes") but only a few [Proverbs](/wiki/Book_of_Proverbs "Book of Proverbs"); and [Isaiah](/wiki/Isaiah "Isaiah") did not write half of the [book of Isaiah](/wiki/Book_of_Isaiah "Book of Isaiah"). The Old Testament was merely a historical record that showed man in a lower state of moral development, with modern man having progressed morally far beyond [Noah](/wiki/Noah "Noah"), [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham"), [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob"), [Judah](/wiki/Judah_(Biblical_figure) "Judah (Biblical figure)"), David, and Solomon. At any rate, according to Briggs, the Scriptures as a whole were riddled with errors and the doctrine of [scriptural inerrancy](/wiki/Biblical_inerrancy "Biblical inerrancy") taught at Princeton Theological Seminary "is a ghost of modern evangelicalism to frighten children."[[4]](#cite_note-Dorrien,_Gary_J.-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Henry_Van_Dyke_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_16229.png/250px-Henry_Van_Dyke_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_16229.png)](/wiki/File:Henry_Van_Dyke_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_16229.png)

[Henry van Dyke](/wiki/Henry_van_Dyke "Henry van Dyke") (1852–1933), a modernist who pushed for revisions to the [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith"), 1900–1910

### The movement to revise the Westminster Confession of Faith, 1900–1910

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The movement to revise the Westminster Confession of Faith, 1900–1910")]

[Henry van Dyke](/wiki/Henry_van_Dyke "Henry van Dyke"), a modernist who had been a major supporter of Briggs in 1893, now headed a movement of modernists and New Schoolers to revise the Westminster Confession of Faith. Since 1889, Van Dyke had been calling for credal revision to affirm that all dying infants (not just [elect](/wiki/Chosen_people "Chosen people") dying infants) go to heaven, to say that God loved the whole world (not just the elect), and to affirm that Christ [atoned](/wiki/Atonement_in_Christianity "Atonement in Christianity") for all mankind, not just the elect. In 1901, he chaired a 25-man committee (with a New School majority). Also in 1901, he drew up a non-binding summary of the church's faith. It mentioned neither biblical inerrancy nor [reprobation](/wiki/Reprobation "Reprobation"), affirmed God's love of all mankind, and denied that the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") was the [Antichrist](/wiki/Antichrist "Antichrist"). It was adopted by General Assembly in 1902 and ratified by the presbyteries in 1904.

As a result of the changes, the Arminian-leaning [Cumberland Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Cumberland_Presbyterian_Church "Cumberland Presbyterian Church") petitioned for reunification, and in 1906, over 1000 Cumberland Presbyterian ministers joined the Presbyterian Church in the USA. The arrival of so many liberal ministers strengthened the New School's position in the church.

## The Doctrinal Deliverance of 1910 (the Five Fundamentals)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The Doctrinal Deliverance of 1910 (the Five Fundamentals)")]

In 1909, there was heated debate in the New York Presbytery about whether or not to ordain three men who refused to assent to the doctrine of the [virgin birth of Jesus](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus"). (They did not deny the doctrine outright but said that they were not prepared to affirm it.) The majority eventually ordained the men; the minority complained to the General Assembly, and it was that complaint that would form the basis of the subsequent controversy. Under the order of the Presbyterian Church in the USA, the General Assembly was not authorized to accept or dismiss the complaint. It should have demitted the complaint to the presbytery and could have done so with instructions that the presbytery hold a heresy trial. The result of the trial could then be appealed to the Synod of New York and from there to the General Assembly. However, the 1910 General Assembly, acting outside its scope of authority, dismissed the complaint against the three men and at the same time instructed its Committee on Bills and Overtures to prepare a statement for governing future ordinations. The committee reported, and the General Assembly passed the Doctrinal Deliverance of 1910, which declared that five doctrines were "necessary and essential" to the Christian faith:

* The [inspiration](/wiki/Biblical_inspiration "Biblical inspiration") of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") by the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit "Holy Spirit") and the [inerrancy](/wiki/Biblical_inerrancy "Biblical inerrancy") of Scripture as a result of this.
* The [virgin birth](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus") of Christ.
* The belief that Christ's death was an [atonement](/wiki/Atonement_in_Christianity "Atonement in Christianity") for sin.
* The bodily [resurrection of Christ](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Christ "Resurrection of Christ").
* The historical reality of [Christ's miracles](/wiki/Miracles_attributed_to_Jesus "Miracles attributed to Jesus").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Sweeney-2005-165-7)

The five propositions would become known to history as the "Five Fundamentals" and by the late 1910s, theological conservatives rallying around the Five Fundamentals came to be known as "fundamentalists".

## *The Fundamentals* and "Back to Fundamentals"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The Fundamentals and \"Back to Fundamentals\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/40/LymanStewart.jpg)](/wiki/File:LymanStewart.jpg)

[Lyman Stewart](/wiki/Lyman_Stewart "Lyman Stewart") (1840–1923), Presbyterian layman and co-founder of [Union Oil](/wiki/Union_Oil "Union Oil"), who funded the publication of *[The Fundamentals](/wiki/The_Fundamentals "The Fundamentals"): A Testimony to the Truth* (1910–1915)

In 1910, a wealthy Presbyterian layman, [Lyman Stewart](/wiki/Lyman_Stewart "Lyman Stewart"), the founder of [Union Oil](/wiki/Union_Oil "Union Oil") and a proponent of [dispensationalism](/wiki/Dispensationalism "Dispensationalism") as taught in the newly published *[Scofield Reference Bible](/wiki/Scofield_Reference_Bible "Scofield Reference Bible")*, decided to use his wealth to sponsor a series of pamphlets to be entitled *[The Fundamentals: A Testimony to the Truth](/wiki/The_Fundamentals:_A_Testimony_To_The_Truth "The Fundamentals: A Testimony To The Truth")*. These twelve pamphlets published between 1910 and 1915 eventually included 90 essays written by 64 authors from several denominations. The series was conservative and critical of [Higher Criticism](/wiki/Higher_Criticism "Higher Criticism") but also broad in its approach, and the scholars who contributed articles included several Presbyterian moderates who would later be opposed to "fundamentalism" such as [Charles R. Erdman Sr.](/wiki/Charles_R._Erdman,_Sr. "Charles R. Erdman, Sr.") and [Robert Elliott Speer](/wiki/Robert_Elliott_Speer "Robert Elliott Speer"). It was apparently from the title of the pamphlets that the term "fundamentalist" was coined, with the first reference to the term being an article by [Northern Baptist](/wiki/Northern_Baptist "Northern Baptist") editor Curtis Lee Laws.

## Ecumenism, 1908–1921

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ecumenism, 1908–1921")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f1/J.G.Machen.jpg)](/wiki/File:J.G.Machen.jpg)

[J. Gresham Machen](/wiki/J._Gresham_Machen "J. Gresham Machen") (1881–1937), founder of the [Orthodox Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Orthodox_Presbyterian_Church "Orthodox Presbyterian Church") and the [Westminster Theological Seminary](/wiki/Westminster_Theological_Seminary "Westminster Theological Seminary")

Several leading Presbyterians, notably Robert E. Speer, played a role in founding the [Federal Council of Churches](/wiki/Federal_Council_of_Churches "Federal Council of Churches") in 1908. This organization (which received 5% of its first year's budget from [John D. Rockefeller Jr.](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller_Jr. "John D. Rockefeller Jr.")) was heavily associated with the [Social Gospel](/wiki/Social_Gospel "Social Gospel"), and with the [Progressive](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era") movement more broadly. The Council's Social Creed of the Churches was adopted by the Presbyterian Church in 1910, but conservatives in General Assembly were able to resist endorsing most of the Council's specific proposals, except for those calling for [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition "Prohibition") and [sabbath laws](/wiki/Blue_law "Blue law").

In response to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the FCC established the General War-Time Commission to coordinate the work of Protestant, Catholic, and Jewish programs related to the war and work closely with the [Department of War](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_War "United States Department of War"). It was chaired by Speer and liberal [Union Theological Seminary](/wiki/Union_Theological_Seminary_(New_York_City) "Union Theological Seminary (New York City)") professor William Adams Brown. Following the war, they worked hard to build on this legacy of unity. The Presbyterian Board of Foreign Missions consequently called for a meeting of Protestant leaders on the topic and in early 1919 the [Interchurch World Movement](/wiki/Interchurch_World_Movement "Interchurch World Movement") (IWM) was established with [John Mott](/wiki/John_Mott "John Mott") as its chairman. The Executive Committee of the Presbyterian Church offered millions of dollars' worth of support to help the IWM with fundraising. When the IWM collapsed financially, the denomination was on the hook for millions of dollars.

However, the debate between modernists and conservatives over the issue of the IWM was small compared to the Church Union debate. In 1919, the General Assembly sent a delegation to a national ecumenical convention that was proposing church union, and in 1920, General Assembly approved a recommendation which included "organic union" with 17 other denominations – the new organization, to be known as the United Churches of Christ in America, would be a sort of "federal government" for member churches: denominations would maintain their distinctive internal identities, but the broader organization would be in charge of things like missions and lobbying for things like prohibition. Under the terms of [presbyterian polity](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity"), the measure would have to be approved by the presbyteries to take effect.

The plans for Church Union were roundly denounced by the Old School Princeton Theological Seminary faculty. It was at this point in 1920 that Princeton professor [J. Gresham Machen](/wiki/J._Gresham_Machen "J. Gresham Machen") first gained prominence within the denomination as a [fundamentalist](/wiki/Fundamentalist_Christianity "Fundamentalist Christianity") opponent of Church Union, which he argued would destroy Presbyterian distinctives, and effectively cede control of the denomination to modernists and their New School allies. However, chinks were starting to show in the Princeton faculty's armor. Charles Erdman and the president of the seminary, William Robinson, came out in favor of the union.

Ultimately, the presbyteries defeated church union by a vote of 150–100 in 1921.

## "Shall the Fundamentalists Win?" (1922)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: \"Shall the Fundamentalists Win?\" (1922)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Harry_Emerson_Fosdick.jpg/250px-Harry_Emerson_Fosdick.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harry_Emerson_Fosdick.jpg)

A 1926 photograph of [Harry Emerson Fosdick](/wiki/Harry_Emerson_Fosdick "Harry Emerson Fosdick") (1878–1969), whose sermon "Shall the Fundamentalists Win?" (1922) sparked the fundamentalist–modernist controversy.

The splits between fundamentalists and modernists had been bubbling in the Presbyterian Church for some time. The event which was to bring the issue to a head was [Harry Emerson Fosdick](/wiki/Harry_Emerson_Fosdick "Harry Emerson Fosdick")'s sermon of May 21, 1922, entitled "Shall the Fundamentalists Win?".[[8]](#cite_note-fosdicksermon-8) Fosdick was an ordained Northern Baptist minister, but had been given authorization to preach in [First Presbyterian Church](/wiki/First_Presbyterian_Church_(Manhattan) "First Presbyterian Church (Manhattan)") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In this sermon, Fosdick presented the liberals in both the Presbyterian Church and the [Northern Baptist Convention](/wiki/American_Baptist_Churches_USA "American Baptist Churches USA") as sincere evangelical Christians who were struggling to reconcile new discoveries in history, science, and religion with the Christian faith. Fundamentalists, on the other hand, were cast as intolerant conservatives who refused to deal with these new discoveries and had arbitrarily drawn the line as to what was off limits in religious discussion. Many people, Fosdick argued, simply found it impossible to accept the virgin birth of Christ, the doctrine of [substitutionary atonement](/wiki/Substitutionary_atonement "Substitutionary atonement"), or the literal [Second Coming](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming") of Christ in the light of modern science. Given the different points of view within the church, only tolerance and liberty could allow for these different perspectives to co-exist in the church.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Conservative [Clarence E. Macartney](/wiki/Clarence_E._Macartney "Clarence E. Macartney"), pastor of [Arch Street Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Arch_Street_Presbyterian_Church "Arch Street Presbyterian Church") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), responded to Fosdick with a sermon of his own, entitled "Shall Unbelief Win?", which was quickly published in a pamphlet. He argued that liberalism had been progressively "secularizing" the church and, if left unchecked, would lead to "a Christianity of opinions and principles and good purposes, but a Christianity without worship, without God, and without Jesus Christ".[[9]](#cite_note-macartneypamphlet-9)

Throughout the proceedings, Fosdick's defense was led by lay elder [John Foster Dulles](/wiki/John_Foster_Dulles "John Foster Dulles").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## William Jennings Bryan and the General Assembly of 1923

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: William Jennings Bryan and the General Assembly of 1923")]

### Background: Darwinism and Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Background: Darwinism and Christianity")]

A giant of Old School Presbyterianism at Princeton, [Charles Hodge](/wiki/Charles_Hodge "Charles Hodge"), was one of the few Presbyterian controversialists to turn their guns on [Darwinism](/wiki/Darwinism "Darwinism") prior to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). Hodge published his *What is Darwinism?* in 1874, three years after *[The Descent of Man](/wiki/The_Descent_of_Man "The Descent of Man")* was published, and argued that if [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin")'s theory excluded the [design argument](/wiki/Teleological_argument "Teleological argument"), it was effectively atheism and could not be reconciled with biblical Christianity.

[Asa Gray](/wiki/Asa_Gray "Asa Gray") responded that Christianity was compatible with Darwin's science. Both he and many other Christians accepted various forms of [theistic evolution](/wiki/Theistic_evolution "Theistic evolution"), and Darwin had not excluded the work of the Creator as a primary cause.[[10]](#cite_note-Gray_74-10)

#### William Jennings Bryan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: William Jennings Bryan")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/William_Jennings_Bryan.JPG/250px-William_Jennings_Bryan.JPG)](/wiki/File:William_Jennings_Bryan.JPG)

[William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan") (1860–1925) in 1907

[William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan"), a former lawyer who had been brought up in the Arminian [Cumberland Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Cumberland_Presbyterian_Church "Cumberland Presbyterian Church") (part of which would merge with the PC-USA in 1906) and who was also a [Presbyterian ruling elder](/wiki/Ruling_elder "Ruling elder"), was elected to Congress in 1890, then became the [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [presidential](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") candidate for three unsuccessful presidential bids in [1896](/wiki/1896_United_States_presidential_election "1896 United States presidential election"), [1900](/wiki/1900_United_States_presidential_election "1900 United States presidential election"), and [1908](/wiki/1908_United_States_presidential_election "1908 United States presidential election"). After his 1900 defeat, Bryan re-examined his life and concluded that he had let his passion for politics obscure his calling as a Christian. Beginning in 1900, he began lecturing on the [Chautauqua](/wiki/Chautauqua "Chautauqua") circuit, where his speeches often involved religious as well as political themes. For the next 25 years until his death, Bryan was one of the most popular Chautauqua lecturers and he spoke in front of hundreds of thousands of people.

By 1905, Bryan had concluded that Darwinism and the modernism of Higher Criticism were allies in promoting liberalism within the church, thereby in his view undermining the foundations of Christianity. In lectures from 1905, Bryan spoke out against the spread of Darwinism, which he characterized as involving "the operation of the law of hate – the merciless law by which the strong crowd out and kill off the weak", and warned that it could undermine the foundations of morality. In 1913, he became [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson")'s [secretary of state](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State"), then resigned in 1915 because he believed that the Wilson administration was about to enter [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in response to the [sinking of the RMS *Lusitania*](/wiki/Sinking_of_the_RMS_Lusitania "Sinking of the RMS Lusitania") and he opposed American intervention in a European war.

When the US did finally join World War I in 1917, Bryan volunteered for the army, though he was never allowed to enlist. At a time of widespread revulsion at alleged German atrocities, Bryan linked evolution to Germany,[[11]](#cite_note-MATHISEN-11) and claimed that Darwinism provided a justification for the strong to dominate the weak and was therefore the source of German [militarism](/wiki/Militarism "Militarism").[[12]](#cite_note-MORAN-12) He drew on reports by the [entomologist](/wiki/Entomologist "Entomologist") [Vernon Kellogg](/wiki/Vernon_Lyman_Kellogg "Vernon Lyman Kellogg") of German officers discussing the Darwinian rationale for their declaration of war,[[13]](#cite_note-ron20s-13) and the [sociologist](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") [Benjamin Kidd](/wiki/Benjamin_Kidd "Benjamin Kidd")'s book *The Science of Power* which contended that [Nietzsche's philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy_of_Friedrich_Nietzsche "Philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche") represented an interpretation of Darwinism,[[12]](#cite_note-MORAN-12) to conclude that [Nietzsche](/wiki/Nietzsche "Nietzsche")'s and Darwin's ideas were the impetus for German [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") and militarism. Bryan argued that Germany's militarism and "barbarism" came from their belief that the "struggle for survival" described in Darwin's *[On the Origin of Species](/wiki/On_the_Origin_of_Species "On the Origin of Species")* applied to nations as well as to individuals,[[12]](#cite_note-MORAN-12) and that "The same science that manufactured poisonous gases to suffocate soldiers is preaching that man has a brute ancestry and eliminating the miraculous and the supernatural from the Bible."[[13]](#cite_note-ron20s-13)

Bryan was, in essence, fighting what would later be called [Social Darwinism](/wiki/Social_Darwinism "Social Darwinism"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) social and economic ideas owing as much to [Herbert Spencer](/wiki/Herbert_Spencer "Herbert Spencer") and [Thomas Malthus](/wiki/Thomas_Malthus "Thomas Malthus") as to Darwin, and viewed by modern biologists as a misuse of his theory.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Germany, or so Bryan's argument ran, had replaced Christ's teachings with Nietzsche's philosophy based on ideas of [survival of the fittest](/wiki/Survival_of_the_fittest "Survival of the fittest"), and the implication was that America would suffer the same fate if unchecked.[[11]](#cite_note-MATHISEN-11) This fear was reinforced by the report of the [psychologist](/wiki/Psychologist "Psychologist") [James H. Leuba](/wiki/James_H._Leuba "James H. Leuba")'s 1916 study indicating that a considerable number of college students lost their faith during the four years they spent in college.

Bryan launched his campaign against Darwinism in 1921 when he was invited to give the James Sprunt Lectures at Virginia's [Union Theological Seminary](/wiki/Union_Presbyterian_Seminary "Union Presbyterian Seminary"). At the end of one, *The Menace of Darwinism*, he said that "Darwinism is not a science at all; it is a string of guesses strung together" and that there is more science in the Bible's "And God said, Let the earth bring forth the living creature..."[[16]](#cite_note-16) than in all of Darwin.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

#### The Auburn Affirmation (1923–1924)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: The Auburn Affirmation (1923–1924)")]

Main article: [Auburn Affirmation](/wiki/Auburn_Affirmation "Auburn Affirmation")

36 clergymen met in [Syracuse, New York](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), and, using Nichols' paper as a base, ultimately issued a declaration known as the [Auburn Affirmation](/wiki/Auburn_Affirmation "Auburn Affirmation").

## The General Assembly of 1924

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: The General Assembly of 1924")]

### Conservative activities prior to the 1924 General Assembly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Conservative activities prior to the 1924 General Assembly")]

The most significant conservative preparation for the General Assembly of 1924 actually occurred slightly before the 1923 General Assembly. This was the publication of [J. Gresham Machen](/wiki/J._Gresham_Machen "J. Gresham Machen")'s *[Christianity and Liberalism](/wiki/Christianity_and_Liberalism "Christianity and Liberalism")*. In this book, Machen argued that liberalism, far from being a set of teachings that could be accommodated within the church, was in fact antithetical to the principles of Christianity and was currently engaged in a struggle against historic Christianity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## The Scopes trial (1925)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The Scopes trial (1925)")]

Main article: [Scopes trial](/wiki/Scopes_trial "Scopes trial")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/HenrySloaneCoffin-TIMEcover_400.jpg/250px-HenrySloaneCoffin-TIMEcover_400.jpg)](/wiki/File:HenrySloaneCoffin-TIMEcover_400.jpg)

[Henry Sloane Coffin](/wiki/Henry_Sloane_Coffin "Henry Sloane Coffin") (1877–1954) on the November 15, 1926 cover of *Time* magazine, during the time that he was president of the Union Theological Seminary, a post he held from 1926-1945. A prominent evangelical liberal, he actively opposed the stances promoted by William Jennings Bryan, such as those presented at the Scopes trial.

At the same time he had been campaigning against Darwinism (largely unsuccessfully) within the Presbyterian Church, William Jennings Bryan had also been encouraging state lawmakers to pass laws banning the teaching of evolution in public schools. Several states had responded to Bryan's call, including [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee"), which passed [such a law](/wiki/Butler_Act "Butler Act") in March 1925.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Given the present-day contours of [the evolution–creation debate](/wiki/Creation%E2%80%93evolution_controversy "Creation–evolution controversy"), in many states in 1925, evolution continued to be taught in church-run institutions at the same time that its teaching was banned in state-run public schools.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [ACLU](/wiki/ACLU "ACLU") was seeking a test case to challenge these anti-evolutionary laws. This led to the famous [trial of John Scopes](/wiki/Scopes_trial "Scopes trial") for teaching evolution in a public school in [Dayton, Tennessee](/wiki/Dayton,_Tennessee "Dayton, Tennessee"). The ACLU sent in renowned [lawyer](/wiki/Lawyer "Lawyer") [John Randolph Neal Jr.](/wiki/John_Randolph_Neal_Jr. "John Randolph Neal Jr.") to defend Scopes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Baptist pastor [William Bell Riley](/wiki/William_Bell_Riley "William Bell Riley"), founder and president of the [World Christian Fundamentals Association](/wiki/World_Christian_Fundamentals_Association "World Christian Fundamentals Association"), persuaded William Jennings Bryan to act as its counsel. Bryan invited his major allies in the Presbyterian General Assembly to attend the trial with him, but J. Gresham Machen refused to testify, saying he had not studied biology in enough detail to testify at trial, while Clarence Macartney had a previous engagement. In response to the announcement that Bryan would be attending the trial, renowned lawyer and committed [agnostic](/wiki/Agnostic "Agnostic") [Clarence Darrow](/wiki/Clarence_Darrow "Clarence Darrow") volunteered to serve on Scopes' defense team.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Foreign missions, 1930–1936

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Foreign missions, 1930–1936")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/John_D._Rockefeller%2C_Jr._%281915%29.jpg/250px-John_D._Rockefeller%2C_Jr._%281915%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_D._Rockefeller,_Jr._(1915).jpg)

[John D. Rockefeller Jr.](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller_Jr. "John D. Rockefeller Jr.") (1874–1960)

In 1930, as a result of widespread second thoughts about missions in general,[[18]](#cite_note-18) a group of [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") laymen at the request of [John D. Rockefeller Jr.](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller_Jr. "John D. Rockefeller Jr.") concluded that it was time for a serious re-evaluation of the effectiveness of foreign missions. With Rockefeller's financial backing, they convinced seven major denominations – the [Methodist Episcopal Church](/wiki/Methodist_Episcopal_Church "Methodist Episcopal Church"), the [Northern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Northern_Baptist_Convention "Northern Baptist Convention"), the [Reformed Church in America](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America"), the [Congregational](/wiki/Congregational_Christian_Churches "Congregational Christian Churches") church, the [Episcopal Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Episcopal Church in the United States of America"), the [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America") and the [United Presbyterian Church of North America](/wiki/United_Presbyterian_Church_of_North_America "United Presbyterian Church of North America") – to participate in their "Laymen's Foreign Missions Inquiry". They commissioned a study of missionaries in [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and launched a separate inquiry under the chairmanship of the philosopher and [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard "Harvard") professor [William Ernest Hocking](/wiki/William_Ernest_Hocking "William Ernest Hocking"). These two inquiries led to the publication of a one-volume summary of the findings of the Laymen's Inquiry entitled *[Re-Thinking Missions: A Laymen's Inquiry After One Hundred Years](/wiki/Re-Thinking_Missions:_A_Laymen%27s_Inquiry_after_One_Hundred_Years "Re-Thinking Missions: A Laymen's Inquiry after One Hundred Years")* in 1932.[[19]](#cite_note-FITZMEIR-19)

*Re-Thinking Missions* argued that in the face of emerging [secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism"), Christians should ally with other world religions, rather than struggle against them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The seven denominations who had agreed to participate in the Laymen's Inquiry now distanced themselves from the report. The [Presbyterian Board of Foreign Missions](/wiki/American_Presbyterian_Mission "American Presbyterian Mission") issued a statement reaffirming the board's commitment to the evangelistic basis of the missionary enterprise and to Jesus Christ as the only Lord and Savior.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Pearl_Buck.jpg/250px-Pearl_Buck.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pearl_Buck.jpg)

[Pearl S. Buck](/wiki/Pearl_S._Buck "Pearl S. Buck") (1892–1973)

[Pearl S. Buck](/wiki/Pearl_S._Buck "Pearl S. Buck") now weighed into the debate. In a review published in *[The Christian Century](/wiki/The_Christian_Century "The Christian Century")*, she praised the report, saying it should be read by every Christian in America and, ironically mimicking the [Biblical literalism](/wiki/Biblical_literalism "Biblical literalism") of the fundamentalists, "I think this is the only book I have ever read that seems to me literally true in its every observation and right in its every conclusion."[[19]](#cite_note-FITZMEIR-19) Then, in a November 1932 speech before a large audience at the [Astor Hotel](/wiki/Hotel_Astor_(New_York) "Hotel Astor (New York)"), later published in *[Harper's](/wiki/Harper%27s "Harper's")*,[[20]](#cite_note-CONN-20) Buck decried gauging the success of missions by the numbers of new church members. Instead she advocated humanitarian efforts to improve the agricultural, educational, medical, and sanitary conditions of the community.[[21]](#cite_note-SMYLIE-21) She described the typical missionary as "narrow, uncharitable, unappreciative, ignorant". In the *Harper's* article along with another in *[Cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitan_(magazine) "Cosmopolitan (magazine)")* published in May 1933, Buck rejected the doctrine of [original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin"), saying "I believe that most of us start out wanting to do right and to be good." She asserted that belief in the [virgin birth](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus") or the [divinity of Christ](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)") was not a prerequisite to being a Christian. She said that the only need is to acknowledge that one cannot live without Christ and to reflect that in one's life.[[20]](#cite_note-CONN-20)[[21]](#cite_note-SMYLIE-21)[[22]](#cite_note-HUTCHISON-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Creation of the Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Creation of the Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions")]

Disapproving of General Assembly's decision not to appoint a new slate of conservatives to the Board of Foreign Missions, [J. Gresham Machen](/wiki/J._Gresham_Machen "J. Gresham Machen"), along with [H. McAllister Griffiths](/w/index.php?title=H._McAllister_Griffiths&action=edit&redlink=1 "H. McAllister Griffiths (page does not exist)"), announced that they were forming an [Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions](/wiki/Independent_Board_for_Presbyterian_Foreign_Missions "Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions") to truly promote biblical and Presbyterian work. Macartney refused to go along with Machen in setting up an independent missions board.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Legacy")]

As a result of the departure of Machen and the denominational conservatives, especially of the Old School, the shape of the Presbyterian Church in the USA as a modernist, liberal denomination was secured. The PCUSA would eventually merge with the [United Presbyterian Church of North America](/wiki/United_Presbyterian_Church_of_North_America "United Presbyterian Church of North America") in 1958 to form the [United Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/United_Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "United Presbyterian Church in the United States of America") and in 1983, the UPCUSA would merge with the [Presbyterian Church in the United States](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States "Presbyterian Church in the United States") (the "Southern Presbyterians" who had split with the PCUSA in 1861 due to the Civil War) to form the current [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The dispute between the fundamentalists and modernists would be played out in nearly every Christian denomination. By the 1920s, it was clear that every mainstream Protestant denomination was going to be willing to accommodate modernism, with the exception of the Presbyterians, Southern Baptists, and the [Missouri Synod Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheran_Church_Missouri_Synod "Lutheran Church Missouri Synod"), where the situation was still unclear. The departure of Machen and other conservatives brought the Presbyterians into the camp willing to accommodate modernism, leaving the [Southern Baptists](/wiki/Southern_Baptists "Southern Baptists") and the Missouri Synod as the only large, national denominations where orthodox Protestants were still active within the denomination. The conflict continued to roil both churches for most of the 20th century and the triumph of orthodox Protestantism in those denominations would not be secure until the [Seminex](/wiki/Seminex "Seminex") schism in the Lutheran church and the [Southern Baptist Convention conservative resurgence](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention_conservative_resurgence "Southern Baptist Convention conservative resurgence") of 1979–1990.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
